next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Venn counted before the bell. The night's new trouble had beaten him to the custodians. Twelve Kondos in the courtyard, twelve names under his breath, twelve bodies he meant to take off this hill the moment the road showed stone again. Arash sat beside him on the wall, long legs stretched out, jaw set toward the valley. There were eleven days until the wedding. Brown floodwater still ate across the switchbacks below, flattening the place where seven roads should have met cleanly. Every morning Venn made the same calculation, and every morning it came out the same: not yet.

The bell rang from the custodians' walkway. It sent a single iron note through stone.

"Everyone," Venn said.

They came at once. Tila came with the cloth wrap of needles and thread in her hand. Habel came with Ori tucked close against her hip, the girl still watching for Asha Mera whenever the families crossed paths. Mava crossed more slowly than the others, with Arash steady at her elbow. After supper the night before, Mava had told him the story in pieces, stopping twice to press her mouth flat before the sound got out. Her sister was Iru. She had been dead for forty years. She was twenty-nine when the cart went off the shrine road and dropped into the ravine. Nushi, the old valley word Iru had used only for Mava, still lived now only when Mava bent over Ori's fever or grief and forgot herself. Then Suna Mera's child had said it in her sleep. Venn had called it coincidence because the other answer would put his family inside someone else's claim.

Ger arrived behind Habel, then Rem and Os...
open_hooks_to_carry: Asha rubbing her left ear in Mava's exact gesture — Suna notices, dismisses, then sees it in Asha's sleep
Asha using a pet name that belongs to Mava — a word from a dialect no one at the shrine speaks
prev_tail_excerpt:
.... They don't know what checkpoints mean.*

She didn't write: *I'm scared.* She didn't need to. The handwriting said it — the hard lean of the letters, the pressure of the pen against the page, the words driving hard and then cutting off.

She closed the journal and put it under her pillow and lay there listening. The flood. The rain starting up again, soft at first and then heavier, drumming on the stone overhead. Ada's small sleeping breaths. A man coughing in the Kondo section. The insects, that constant monsoon hum from the wet vegetation around the shrine, a sound so persistent you stopped hearing it until the night got quiet enough to bring it back.

There were forty-three of them on the hilltop, four days in, with no road, no phone, and nobody coming for them. Morning would come the way it did here now: people stirring before dawn, the bell sooner or later, everyone getting up to carry water, peel onions, clean, eat. By noon the courtyard would be full of kids crossing from one family to another with dirty knees and wet cuffs, like nobody had told them where the borders were supposed to be. The adults would watch and decide, depending on the minute, whether that felt like comfort or trouble.

Leni bit her thumbnail in the dark. She could taste stone dust and onion and the faint metallic residue of the pen she'd been chewing without noticing.

Day 5 would look like that. Then Day 6. Then however many days until the water went down or the checkpoints opened or someone, somewhere, figured out they were up here.

She didn't think anyone was figuring out they were up here.
state_deltas_required: Asha manifesting Mava Kondo's gesture and vocabulary — the first crack in the rational order
a connection between the Mera and Kondo families is established through a four-year-old's body; Suna's worldview absorbs another fracture; Mava's private grief (dead sister) is made visible
